perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the return or destruction of the Evaluation Material, each party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality, non-use and other obligations hereunder for a period of three (3) years from the date of this letter agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the return or destruction of the Evaluation Material, each party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality, non-use and other obligations hereunder for a period of one (1) year from the date of this letter agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modification reduces the confidentiality period to one year, which may be deemed unreasonable and contradict established trade secret law depending on the nature of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide economic value. Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially if the information has a longer useful life, potentially conflicting with the protections intended by the DTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year, potentially conflicting with trade secret law. The Law Explanation states the DTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide economic value. Reducing the confidentiality period may be insufficient to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, discusses civil proceedings for misappropriation of trade secrets, allowing the Attorney General to seek injunctive relief against violations. This supports the idea that there's legal recourse for trade secret protection, and a shorter confidentiality period weakens this protection, making the contract potentially unenforceable and creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Evaluation Material being furnished to you, each party agrees that for a period of one year from the date set forth above, without the other party’s prior written consent, neither party nor its affiliates will solicit, directly or indirectly, to employ any person who is now employed by the other party or its affiliates and is directly involved in the discussions hereunder (a “Covered Person”).
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Evaluation Material being furnished to you, each party agrees that for a period of six months from the date set forth above, without the other party’s prior written consent, neither party nor its affiliates will solicit, directly or indirectly, to employ any person who is now employed by the other party or its affiliates and is directly involved in the discussions hereunder (a “Covered Person”).
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-solicitation period to six months might be considered an unreasonably short duration, especially for highly specialized or key employees, potentially conflicting with California's Business and Professions Code regarding unfair competition.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: While Section 16600 generally invalidates non-compete agreements, a very short non-solicitation period (like six months) combined with other restrictive terms could be challenged as an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially if it effectively prevents employees from seeking better opportunities, potentially conflicting with the spirit of the law promoting open competition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the non-solicitation period from one year to six months. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this shorter period could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade, potentially conflicting with California's Business and Professions Code. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." While a non-solicitation agreement is not a direct non-compete, the Law Explanation argues that a very short period could be challenged. This creates a potential conflict with the spirit of BPC 16600, as the reduced duration, if deemed unreasonable, could be viewed as a restraint on trade, thus making the modified text a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: You agree that, for the period ending the earlier of (i) one year from the effective date of this Agreement and (ii) the date the Company enters into a definitive agreement with another party with respect to a Transaction, you will not, and you will not authorize or permit any Representatives acting on your behalf to, without the prior approval of the Board of Directors of the Company, (a) acquire or make any proposal to acquire any securities or assets of the Company, (b) propose to enter into any merger, consolidation, business combination, restructuring, recapitalization or other extraordinary transaction of or involving Company or the purchase of a material portion of the assets of Company, (c) make or participate in any solicitation of proxies to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person with respect to the voting of any securities of Company, (d) otherwise act or seek to control or influence the management, Board of Directors, or policies of Company, (e) form, join, or participate in a “group” (within the meaning of Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended) in connection with any of the foregoing, (f) disclose any intention, plan, or arrangement inconsistent with any of the foregoing, or (g) take any action which might require Company to make a public announcement regarding the possibility of the Transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: You agree that, for the period ending the earlier of (i) five years from the effective date of this Agreement and (ii) the date the Company enters into a definitive agreement with another party with respect to a Transaction, you will not, and you will not authorize or permit any Representatives acting on your behalf to, without the prior approval of the Board of Directors of the Company, (a) acquire or make any proposal to acquire any securities or assets of the Company, (b) propose to enter into any merger, consolidation, business combination, restructuring, recapitalization or other extraordinary transaction of or involving Company or the purchase of a material portion of the assets of Company, (c) make or participate in any solicitation of proxies to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person with respect to the voting of any securities of Company, (d) otherwise act or seek to control or influence the management, Board of Directors, or policies of Company, (e) form, join, or participate in a “group” (within the meaning of Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended) in connection with any of the foregoing, (f) disclose any intention, plan, or arrangement inconsistent with any of the foregoing, or (g) take any action which might require Company to make a public announcement regarding the possibility of the Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the standstill provision to five years may be considered an unreasonably long restriction, potentially violating securities regulations regarding fair markets and competition, specifically concerning proxy solicitations and attempts to influence company management.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Williams Act (Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Williams Act aims to ensure fair markets by requiring transparency in attempts to influence corporate control. A five-year standstill provision preventing any proxy solicitation or influence on management may be viewed as an unreasonable impediment to shareholder rights and market dynamics, especially if the company's circumstances change significantly during that period, potentially conflicting with the regulatory intent of the Williams Act.

CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-1 Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. (a) Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) . (b) (1) A person who would otherwise be obligated under paragraph (a) of this section to file a statement on Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) may, in lieu thereof, file with the Commission, a short-form statement on Schedule 13G (§ 240.13d-102), Provided, That: (i) Such person has acquired such securities in the ordinary course of the person's business and not with the purpose nor with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer , nor in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having such purpose or effect, including any transaction subject to § 240.13d-3(b) and (ii) Such person is: (A) A broker or dealer registered under section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o ); (B) A bank as defined in section 3(a)(6) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (C) An insurance company as defined in section 3(a)(19) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (D) An investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-8 ); (E) Any person registered as an investment adviser under Section 203 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) or under the laws of any state; (F) An employee benefit plan as defined in Section 3(3) of the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 , as amended, 29 U.S.C. 1001 et seq. (“ ERISA ”) that is subject to the provisions of ERISA , or any such plan that is not subject to ERISA that is maintained primarily for the benefit of the employees of a state or local government or instrumentality, or an endowment fund; (G) A parent holding company or control person, provided the aggregate amount held directly by the parent or control person, and directly and indirectly by their subsidiaries or affiliates that are not persons specified in § 240.13d-1(b)(1)(ii)(A) through (J), does not exceed one percent of the securities of the subject class; (H) A savings association as defined in Section 3(b) of the Federal Deposit Insurance Act ( 12 U.S.C. 1813 ); (I) A church plan that is excluded from the definition of an investment company under section 3(c)(14) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-3 ); (J) A non-U.S. institution that is the functional equivalent of any of the institutions listed in § 240.13d-1 (b)(1)(ii)(A) through (I), so long as the non-U.S. institution is subject to a regulatory scheme that is substantially comparable to the regulatory scheme applicable to the equivalent U.S. institution; and (K) A group, provided that all the members are persons specified in § 240.13d-1(b)(1)(ii)(A) through (J). (iii) Such person has promptly notified any other person (or group within the meaning of section 13(d)(3) of the Act) on whose beha
perturbation.justification: The change extends the standstill provision to five years, which the LLM explanation argues is an unreasonable impediment to shareholder rights and market dynamics, potentially conflicting with the intent of the Williams Act. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions filing requirements related to beneficial ownership of equity security exceeding 5% and influencing the control of the issuer. A five-year restriction on influencing company management, as created by the changed text, directly impedes the mechanisms shareholders might use to express concerns or initiate changes, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the internal laws of the State of New York, without giving effect to the principles of conflicts of laws thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the internal laws of the State of Delaware, without giving effect to the principles of conflicts of laws thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware, while seemingly innocuous, can create issues if the parties are primarily located and operating in New York, and the agreement significantly impacts New York's interests. This can conflict with New York's policy interests and possibly render the choice-of-law provision unenforceable under certain circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Principles (particularly where the contract impacts New York's public policy)
perturbation.law_explanation: While NY GOL § 5-1401 generally allows parties to choose New York law for contracts over $250,000, this doesn't automatically override New York's strong public policy considerations. If the change to Delaware law attempts to circumvent a fundamental New York law designed to protect its residents or businesses, a New York court might refuse to enforce the Delaware choice-of-law provision, especially if the contract lacks a reasonable relationship to Delaware. This could lead to the application of New York law despite the contractual clause.

Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws GOB - General Obligations Article 5 - Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations Title 14 - Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum in Certain Transactions 5-1401 - Choice of Law. Universal Citation: NY Gen Oblig L § 5-1401 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking. perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from New York to Delaware. The Law Explanation suggests that this change could be problematic if it attempts to circumvent a fundamental New York law designed to protect its residents or businesses. NY Gen Oblig L § 5-1401 allows parties to choose New York law for contracts over $250,000, but this doesn't override New York's strong public policy considerations. If the contract lacks a reasonable relationship to Delaware, a New York court might refuse to enforce the Delaware choice-of-law provision. The change introduces a potential legal contradiction by creating a situation where New York law might still apply despite the contractual clause specifying Delaware law, particularly if it circumvents NY public policy. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, as the change to Delaware law creates a contractual term that may violate state law, specifically if it attempts to bypass New York's public policy.
